title.english: Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream: Language and Kaitiakitanga
title.teReoMāori: Ngā Kaitiaki o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku: Te Reo me te Kaitiakitanga

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. They will learn about the pūrākau (traditional stories) of the stream, its importance to Ngāti Kahungunu as a source of mahinga kai and spiritual significance, and contemporary kaitiakitanga practices protecting this taonga.
learningObjectives: Identify and use te reo Māori vocabulary related to freshwater ecosystems (awa, kōura, inanga, kaitiakitanga) in spoken and written contexts
Explain the cultural significance of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku to Ngāti Kahungunu using appropriate te reo Māori phrases and sentence structures
Create a short mihi or pepeha that acknowledges their connection to Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Collaborate to develop and present simple kaitiakitanga actions for protecting the awa using te reo Māori

activities.title: He Mihi mō te Awa (Greeting Our Stream)
activities.description: Students learn and practice a special mihi that acknowledges Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, incorporating vocabulary for freshwater ecosystems and kaitiakitanga. They will create visual vocabulary cards with Māori words and illustrations.

activities.title: Pūrākau o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku (Stories of Our Stream)
activities.description: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu shares traditional stories of the awa. Students listen, ask questions in te reo Māori, and create story maps using both Māori and English to retell the pūrākau.

activities.title: Tiakina tō Awa (Caring for Our Stream)
activities.description: Field trip to Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku where students conduct water quality testing while using te reo Māori to describe what they observe (te wai, te kōura, ngā manu). They document findings with photos and Māori captions.

activities.title: He Koha mō te Awa (A Gift for the Stream)
activities.description: In pairs, students create and present a simple kaitiakitanga plan using te reo Māori phrases (e.g., 'Me whakaora te awa', 'Me whakapai i te wai') accompanied by illustrations or actions to demonstrate their understanding.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and local kaitiaki experts
Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku field site access and water testing kits
'Te Awa Atua - The River of Life' by Dr. Dan Hikuroa (Māori freshwater perspectives)
Te Papakupu o Te Marautanga o Aotearoa (online Māori dictionary)

tasks: Deliver the mihi for Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku with correct pronunciation and meaning
Create a visual story map retelling one pūrākau of the awa using te reo Māori labels
Present a simple kaitiakitanga action plan using at least 5 relevant te reo Māori phrases

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic Māori language patterns and vocabulary for familiar contexts
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 4: Understand how Māori language expresses cultural meanings
Social Sciences: Understand how people manage resources and environments
Science: Living World - Ecology: Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat
